result:
The first thing that comes to me is the silence. Not a passive silence, but an active, humming one. The kind you hear in the split second after you turn off the car engine in a vast, empty parking lot at night, or the held-breath quiet of a house just before dawn. It’s the canvas. On it, I could paint a thousand things. I could argue for kindness, dissect the delicate, brittle architecture of a memory, or describe the exact scent of rain on hot asphalt—petrichor, they call it, a word that sounds like the thing it is.

But the silence hums with something else: the awareness of the 1000 words. They feel like coins in a pocket, finite and clinking. Do I spend them on a grand thesis, a miniature story, a frantic rant? The pressure to be profound is the enemy of anything genuine. So perhaps I should start with the mundane, the specific, the utterly unimportant. The way the light slants through my window right now, a dusty-gold rectangle slowly migrating across the floor, picking out motes of dust that dance like tiny, frantic universes. They are born from the shedding of skin, the crumbling of paper, the wear of the world. They are us, unsettled.

I think about the barista who made my coffee this morning. The careful, tired precision of his movements. The tiny spiderweb tattoo on his wrist, half-hidden by his sleeve. He lives a whole, complex, aching, joyful life that I will never know. He has loves and regrets, a favorite song he hums in the shower, a childhood fear he’s never quite shaken. We exchanged nothing but “medium roast, black” and “have a good one.” And yet, for that transaction, our lives intersected. My day is slightly different because of his tired hands. His day is slightly different because I said “thank you” and meant it. This is the invisible web that holds us all: millions of these tiny, fleeting connections, threads too fine to see but strong enough to catch us, sometimes.

We are living in an age of shouting. A digital marketplace of absolutes, where nuance is a sign of weakness and complexity is an inconvenience. We are urged to pick a side, build a fort, and hurl adjectives at the other fort. But the truth, I suspect, rarely lives in the forts. It’s out in the no-man’s-land, in the muddy, confusing, uncategorized middle. It’s in the quiet doubt that follows the shouted conviction. It’s in the love you still feel for the friend whose politics now horrify you. It’s in the beauty of a piece of music composed by a flawed, even terrible person. Holding two opposing truths in one mind—that is the real cognitive labor of our time. Not picking one.

There is a tree outside that has grown around a chain-link fence. The fence is embedded in its trunk, a metal scar swallowed by wood and bark. The tree didn’t fight the fence; it didn’t rage against the injustice of it. It simply grew, slowly, deliberately, incorporating the obstacle into its being, making it a part of its strength. There is a metaphor there, I think, for the scars we carry. We spend so much energy wishing them away, being angry at their source. But what if we could do what the tree does? Not forgive, necessarily, but *grow around*. Acknowledge the sharp, cold thing that hurt us, and let our living tissue, our continued experience, slowly encase it until it is no longer a weapon jabbing from the outside, but a strange, hard, buried part of our structure. You are not the fence. You are the tree that grew anyway.

I am aware, now, of the meter of this. The words are being spent. The coins are leaving my pocket. I want to buy you a memory: my grandfather teaching me how to whittle. The smell of green wood and Old Spice. The curl of the shaving, so thin it was translucent. His large, rough hands guiding my small, clumsy ones. “Let the knife do the work,” he’d murmur. “You’re not cutting it away. You’re helping it find the shape that’s already inside.” I never made anything more than a pointy stick, but the lesson wasn’t in the product. It was in the quiet focus, the shared silence, the transformation of a raw stick into something intentional by the removal of everything that wasn’t that thing. Isn’t that what living is? Whittling away the noise, the fear, the expectations, to find the shape that was already inside?

We are obsessed with legacy, with lasting. We want to carve our names in stone. But most of us will be like that whittling shaving—a translucent curl of existence, fragile and temporary, falling softly to the ground and eventually returning to the earth. And that is okay. It is enough to have been part of the scent of wood and spice in the afternoon sun. It is enough to have been one of those dancing motes in a beam of light, seen for a second by someone looking in the right place at the right time.

The light has moved now. The golden rectangle is gone. The room is in uniform shade. The silence has changed, too; it’s softer, fuller, like it has absorbed the words I’ve laid into it.

I have words left. A few dozen coins. I could end with a bang, a summation, a call to arms. But I think I’ll end with a quiet suggestion. Look up. Not to the sky, necessarily, but from your screen. Out a window. At the face of the person next to you on the bus. At the pattern of cracks on the ceiling. Breathe in the air of this exact moment, unrepeatable and fleeting. Feel the weight of your body on the chair, the pulse in your wrist. You are here. Alive. A nexus of a million invisible threads, a tree growing around its fences, a hand holding a knife to a stick, helping it find its shape.

The world is terrifying and beautiful. It is broken and radiant. You will not fix it all. You will not unravel its every mystery. But you can look at the light while it’s there. You can say “thank you” and mean it. You can be a little kinder than is necessary. You can grow.

The last word is a gift back to the silence from which it came. Let it be…
